skill.name: when-managing-github-projects-use-github-project-management
skill.description: Automates GitHub project management workflows with issue triage, sprint planning, milestone tracking, project board updates, and stakeholder reporting through coordinated agents.
skill.summary: Coordinate GitHub issues, sprints, milestones, and Projects v2 boards with agent-assisted workflows.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command-execution patterns, but review shows they are documented shell and MCP examples for GitHub project management rather than hidden executable code. No prompt injection, hardcoded credential exfiltration, or malicious network endpoint was found. The skill still carries medium risk because it directs agents to run local scripts, read local planning files, and sync project data with GitHub, Jira, and Slack.

medium_findings.title: Agent-Directed Shell Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill contains many examples that instruct agents to run npx, bash scripts, and MCP-style commands for project automation. This appears intentional for a GitHub project management skill, but execution can mutate repositories, milestones, labels, releases, and project boards if used without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and repeated, so the capability is real. The context is legitimate project automation, so this is operational risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: External Service Synchronization Can Share Project Data
medium_findings.description: The skill describes bidirectional Jira synchronization and Slack webhook notifications. No hardcoded exfiltration endpoint was found, but project metadata, issue details, and milestone status could be sent to third-party systems configured by the user.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The external integrations are clearly documented and use placeholders rather than hidden endpoints. The data-sharing risk depends on user configuration and repository sensitivity.

medium_findings.title: Local File Content Used In GitHub API Arguments
medium_findings.description: A milestone creation example uses shell command substitution to read a local release plan into a GitHub milestone description. This is a reasonable workflow, but it can publish local planning content to GitHub if the file contains sensitive information.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell substitution is visible and tied to a local release-plan file. It is not obfuscated, but it can disclose local file contents to GitHub by design.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptographic algorithm patterns at several lines, but the reviewed lines contain descriptive project-management text, claude-flow alpha package references, or a shell example. No cryptographic implementation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows no MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or similar cryptographic use. The alerts are keyword collisions in ordinary documentation and command examples.

low_findings.title: Static Reconnaissance Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged reconnaissance in the skill and diagram, but the reviewed lines describe team size, sprint flow, and risk identification in a project-management process. No host, network, or system enumeration instructions were found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are project-management prose and graph edges. They do not instruct scanning hosts, probing networks, or collecting system information.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Commands In Skill Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes shell command blocks that run npx and local bash scripts. Users should inspect referenced scripts and confirm repository targets before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell command pattern is directly present in code blocks. Risk is bounded because commands are documented and mostly parameterized.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Substitution In API Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The milestone example uses command substitution to include a local release-plan file in an API command. This can publish local content if used with sensitive files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The substitution syntax is explicit and the destination is a GitHub milestone command. The impact depends on the contents of the referenced file.

content.user_title: Manage GitHub Projects With Agent Workflows
content.value_statement: GitHub project management often spreads work across issues, milestones, releases, and status updates. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured workflows for triage, sprint planning, board sync, and reporting.
actual_capabilities: Defines a coordinator-led workflow for GitHub issue triage, labeling, assignment, and priority scoring.
Guides sprint planning with team capacity checks, backlog review, milestone creation, and sprint board setup.
Supports release and milestone tracking with progress reports, risk review, release readiness checks, and stakeholder updates.
Describes GitHub Projects v2 custom field automation for priority, complexity, cycle time, review status, and team ownership.
Coordinates specialized agents for planning, issue tracking, board synchronization, and workflow orchestration.
Includes a process diagram that maps issue triage, sprint planning, milestone tracking, and external tool sync.
limitations: The referenced scripts and reference files are named but not included in this skill package.
Users must provide valid GitHub, Jira, Slack, and project identifiers before workflows can run.
The skill can suggest repository changes, but humans should review destructive or public-facing actions.
Automation quality depends on the repository label system, team ownership data, and issue hygiene.

use_cases.title: Triage a growing issue backlog
use_cases.description: Classify new issues, apply labels, identify duplicates, assign owners, and place work on the right project board.

use_cases.title: Plan a focused development sprint
use_cases.description: Review capacity, group backlog items, create a sprint milestone, and initialize a sprint board with clear ownership.

use_cases.title: Track a release milestone
use_cases.description: Break release goals into issues, monitor progress, flag risks, and prepare stakeholder reports before release.

prompt_templates.title: Start issue triage
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow for a repository with new issues.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to triage open issues in <owner/repo>. Classify each issue, suggest labels, detect duplicates, and propose assignees. Do not change GitHub until I approve the plan.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare sprint planning
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate workflow for creating a sprint proposal.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to prepare Sprint <number> for <team>. Review the backlog, estimate capacity for <dates>, group related work, and propose a sprint goal with issue candidates.

prompt_templates.title: Sync project board state
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow for operational board maintenance.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to audit GitHub Projects v2 board <project>. Compare issue status, PR activity, blocked labels, milestones, and custom fields. Provide a change list for approval.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate a release milestone
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow for release tracking and communication.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to manage milestone <milestone> in <owner/repo>. Identify risks, check release readiness, generate a stakeholder update, and list any GitHub changes that need approval.

output_examples.input: Triage issues opened this week for a web app repository.
output: A table of issues grouped by bug, feature, documentation, and question.
Recommended labels, owners, priority, and duplicate candidates for each issue.
A review queue of proposed GitHub updates that require approval before execution.

output_examples.input: Plan the next two-week sprint for the platform team.
output: A sprint goal linked to capacity assumptions and team availability.
A proposed sprint backlog with issue groups, dependencies, and risk notes.
Suggested milestone and project board updates for review.
A milestone health summary with completed work, blocked items, and deadline risks.
A release checklist status with documentation, CI, PR review, and notes coverage.
A concise stakeholder update suitable for project communication.

best_practices: Run workflows in review mode first, then approve specific GitHub changes after checking repository targets.
Keep label taxonomy, team ownership, capacity data, and board templates current before running automation.
Limit tokens and webhooks to the repositories, projects, and external channels required for the task.
anti_patterns: Do not run referenced scripts from an untrusted repository without reading them first.
Do not push issue details to Slack or Jira unless the destination is approved for that project data.
Do not let the agent create releases, milestones, or labels without human review in production repositories.

faq.question: Does this skill include working GitHub API scripts?
faq.answer: No. It references script names and workflows, but the script files are not included in the package.

faq.question: Can it update GitHub Projects v2 boards?
faq.answer: It provides workflows and command patterns for Projects v2 updates. Users must provide working API tooling and credentials.

faq.question: Is this safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: Use it with care. Review every proposed command and avoid syncing private issue details to unapproved external systems.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The package declares support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it replace a project manager?
faq.answer: No. It structures routine triage, planning, and reporting work, but humans should set priorities and approve changes.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it instructs agents to run commands and change GitHub project state.
